coredata.dc:title: Ternary CD/Co3O4@MnCo2O4 heterojunction nanozymes for enhanced sonodynamic/NIR–II–photothermal/nanocatalytic therapy through triple amplification of ROS generation

coredata.dc:description: The therapeutic potential of nanozyme-based tumor catalytic therapy (NCT) has been widely recognized, yet its efficacy is compromised by the low-efficiency single-component nanozymes and multiple factors within the tumor microenvironment (TME) including adequate levels of H2O2 and overexpressed GSH milieu. Herein, the mild NIR-II hyperthermia and heterojunction fabrication co-augmented NCT strategy are first presented to realize the triple amplification of ROS generation. To demonstrate this concept, a novel ternary heterojunction nanozyme termed CD/Co3O4@MnCo2O4 (CD/CMCO) is successfully constructed through depositing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) on core-shell structural Co3O4@MnCo2O4 Z-scheme heterojunctions. The formed ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions could be adopted as a US and NIR-II light double-responsive theranostic nanoplatform for simultaneous sonodynamic/NIR–II–photothermal/nanocatalytic therapy. Notably, the sonodynamic and NIR-II photothermal performances as well as the triple enzyme-like catalytic activities of Co3O4@MnCo2O4 and CDs can be enhanced by the construction of ternary heterojunctions owing to the optimized separation and migration of carriers. More interestingly, CD/CMCO with outstanding NIR-II photothermal characteristics enables the attainment of moderately elevated temperatures (∼43 °C), further amplifying the catalytic activities and then improving ROS generation. The triple cascade amplification of ROS generation is achieved by CD/CMCO with exceptional enzyme-like catalytic activities, which produced more ROS through alleviating hypoxia and consuming GSH. These favorable advantages of CD/CMCO enable it to be a triple ROS generation accelerator for heterojunction and mild NIR-II hyperthermia co-augmented synergistic tumor therapy. This work paves a novel avenue for exploring efficient tumor treatment based on ternary heterojunction nanozymes that are responsive to US and NIR-II light.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of CD/CMCO ternary heterojunctions. (a–d) TEM and HRTEM images of CMCO and CD/CMCO. (e–i) Survey XPS (e), high-resolution Mn 2p (f), Co 2p (g), O 1s (h), N 1s (i) spectra of CD/CMCO. (j) XRD patterns, (k) Absorption spectra, (l) FTIR spectra of CD, CMCO, and CD/CMCO. (m) Element mapping images of CD/CMCO.

Ternary heterojunction enhanced sonodynamic properties of CD/CMCO. (a–e) ROS generation efficiency evaluations of CMCO, CDs, and CD/CMCO using absorption spectrum and ESR spectrum. (f–i) Tauc plot and XPS-VB spectra of Co3O4, MnCo2O4 and CDs. (j) The energy band diagrams of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions were schematically illustrated.

Enhanced photothermal performances of CD/CMCO. (a–d) When subjected to 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation at various concentrations and laser power densities, the temperature of the CD/CMCO solution showed fluctuations. (e, f) Evaluation of photothermal stability of CD/CMCO. (g, h) Investigating the temperature change of CD/CMCO, CMCO, and CD solution when subjected to 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation. (i, j) Evaluation of conversion efficiency in CD/CMCO. (k) Images of CD/CMCO solution under 1064 nm irradiation.

Enhanced triple enzyme-like catalytic activities of CD/CMCO. (a–h) Measurement and comparison of POD-like activity of CMCO, CD/CMCO, and CD/CMCO + 1064 nm at pH 6.0 or 6.5. (i–l) Evaluation of GSH-px-like and CAT-like catalytic activities of CMCO and CD/CMCO.

In vitro antitumor effectiveness of CD/CMCO. (a, b) Cytotoxicity evaluation of CD/CMCO against LO2 or CT26 cells. (c) Cytotoxicity evaluation of CD/CMCO against CT26 cells under US irradiation in the absence or presence of mild NIR-II laser irradiation. (e, f) Live/dead and ROS staining evaluation of different treatments against CT26 cells. (g) Mitochondrial membrane potential analysis of CT-26 cells in different groups.

In vivo antitumor effectiveness of CD/CMCO. (a) Diagram showing the in vitro anticancer treatment of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions. (b–e) NIR fluorescence imaging of CT26 tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of CD/CMCO. (f–i) Body weight, tumor weight, tumor volume, and survival rate of mice after different treatments. (j, k) H&E and TUNEL staining of tumors after different treatments.
Schematic illustration of CD/CMCO construction for heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II hyperthermia-enhanced SDT and NCT.

head.ce:title: Ternary CD/Co3O4@MnCo2O4 heterojunction nanozymes for enhanced sonodynamic/NIR–II–photothermal/nanocatalytic therapy through triple amplification of ROS generation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The therapeutic potential of nanozyme-based tumor catalytic therapy (NCT) has been widely recognized, yet its efficacy is compromised by the low-efficiency single-component nanozymes and multiple factors within the tumor microenvironment (TME) including adequate levels of H2O2 and overexpressed GSH milieu. Herein, the mild NIR-II hyperthermia and heterojunction fabrication co-augmented NCT strategy are first presented to realize the triple amplification of ROS generation. To demonstrate this concept, a novel ternary heterojunction nanozyme termed CD/Co3O4@MnCo2O4 (CD/CMCO) is successfully constructed through depositing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) on core-shell structural Co3O4@MnCo2O4 Z-scheme heterojunctions. The formed ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions could be adopted as a US and NIR-II light double-responsive theranostic nanoplatform for simultaneous sonodynamic/NIR–II–photothermal/nanocatalytic therapy. Notably, the sonodynamic and NIR-II photothermal performances as well as the triple enzyme-like catalytic activities of Co3O4@MnCo2O4 and CDs can be enhanced by the construction of ternary heterojunctions owing to the optimized separation and migration of carriers. More interestingly, CD/CMCO with outstanding NIR-II photothermal characteristics enables the attainment of moderately elevated temperatures (∼43 °C), further amplifying the catalytic activities and then improving ROS generation. The triple cascade amplification of ROS generation is achieved by CD/CMCO with exceptional enzyme-like catalytic activities, which produced more ROS through alleviating hypoxia and consuming GSH. These favorable advantages of CD/CMCO enable it to be a triple ROS generation accelerator for heterojunction and mild NIR-II hyperthermia co-augmented synergistic tumor therapy. This work paves a novel avenue for exploring efficient tumor treatment based on ternary heterojunction nanozymes that are responsive to US and NIR-II light.

Overall scheme to show the main synthesis procedures of CD/CMCO and their applications for heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II hyperthermia-enhanced SDT and NCT.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Health serves as the cornerstone of individuals' well-being, yet cancer has emerged as a significant threat to people's health [1]. With the fast progress of theranostic nanomedicine, a wide range of therapeutic methods for cancer treatment have emerged, such as reactive oxygen species (ROS)-based treatment modalities [2–6]. Nanozymes, also known as catalytically active nanomaterials, have been regarded as potential substitutes for natural enzymes owing to the following advancements including easy acquirement, tunable catalytic activities, and outstanding stability [7–11]. Specifically, nanozymes can perform catalytic therapy in the tumor microenvironment (TME) by acting as peroxidase (POD) mimics and producing highly cytotoxic ROS for nanozymes-based catalytic therapy (NCT) [12–15]. However, the TME's highly complex features (including the low H2O2 concentration and high GSH level) lead to a low catalytic efficiency of nanozymes, thereby severely limiting their performance in battling tumors [16–19]. Therefore, improving the catalytic efficiency of nanozymes for effective tumor treatment is both urgent and difficult.

The first strategy to improve the catalytic activity of nanozymes is to use the Arrhenius equation, which has been demonstrated to increase the catalytic activity of nanozymes with increasing temperature [20–25]. By utilizing near-infrared light (NIR), photothermal therapy (PTT) effectively produces localized hyperthermia for tumor ablation, which can overcome the temperature limitations associated with NCT [26–32]. As a new approach, the integration of nanozymes for NCT with PTT has the potential to enhance the catalytical production of ROS and reduce the adverse effects caused by high power density light irradiation simultaneously [33–36]. On this basis, there have been some newly developed nanozymes that combine photothermal and catalytic activities for synergistic tumor therapy, such as metallic oxide and carbon-based nanozymes [37–40]. Nevertheless, PTT-assisted NCT often encounters two bottlenecks. On the one hand, current light-induced nanozymes are mainly designed for low-efficiency NIR-I light, which has limited tissue penetration depth and low maximum permissible exposure, making them less suitable for in vivo applications [41–43]. In contrast, NIR-II light has distinct advantages over NIR-I light, including greater tissue penetration and a higher threshold for skin tolerance [44,45]. On the other hand, the constant “always-on” feature and the excessive operating temperature (>50 °C) of PTT may lead to irreparable harm to healthy tissues around the tumor even within the NIR-II range [46–48]. If the photothermal effect is decreased, however, the effectiveness of PTT is significantly diminished. Hence, we hypothesized that mild hyperthermia (approximately 43 °C) at NIR-II window could serve as a supplementary therapeutic approach, as opposed to directly destroying cancer cells. As expected, mild hyperthermia can not only enhance the catalytic activity of nanozymes, but also alleviate tumor hypoxia through increasing blood circulation. Hence, endowing multifunctional nanozymes with exceptional NIR-II photothermal characteristics may facilitate NCT through mild hyperthermia, thus addressing the limitations of a single mode.

The second strategy to enhance the therapeutic effect of NCT is to integrate it with other ROS-mediated therapy modalities, such as photodynamic (PDT) and sonodynamic therapy (SDT) [16,49–51]. Compared to PDT with limited penetration depth, the use of exogenous ultrasound-activated SDT to create ROS is gaining popularity because of its deeper tissue penetration (over 10 cm) [52,53]. Despite this, the effectiveness of SDT is still hindered by the low-efficiency sonosensitizers and complicated TME [54–57]. The inefficacy of organic sonosensitizers in SDT is ascribed to their limited water solubility and photostability [58–60]. Albeit inorganic sonosensitizers represented by TiO2 nanostructures offer improved chemical stability and a modifiable energy bandgap, the tendency for generated electrons and holes to recombine easily within the structure severely restrain their application prospects for SDT [61–64]. Consequently, it is imperative to seek a high-efficiency sonosensitizer with narrow bandgap and inhibited electron-hole recombination to overcome these barriers for enhanced SDT. Based on this situation, the construction of heterostructures containing nanozyme components with NIR-II photothermal effects can not only regulate electron-hole separation kinetics and thereby amplify ROS production, but also alleviate tumor hypoxia and further enhance sonodynamic activity.

Currently, it is still a challenge to endow a theranostic nanoplatform with triple functions including sonodynamic performances, triple enzyme-like catalytic activities, and NIR-II photothermal conversion properties. To substantiate the hypothesis proposed above, in this work, we report for the first time the design of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions that incorporates carbon dots (CDs) with excellent NIR-II photothermal properties and core-shell structural Co3O4@MnCo2O4 (CMCO) Z-scheme heterojunctions with sonodynamic and multiple enzyme-like catalytic activities (Scheme 1 ). The hollow CMCO was synthesized by the template-etching strategy, which exhibited excellent sonodynamic and catalytic activities owing to the formation of Z-scheme heterojunctions. The sonodynamic and catalytic activities of CMCO were further amplified by depositing CDs, achieving the first-level amplification of ROS production due to the prevention of electron-hole pair recombination and the acceleration of carrier transfer. In addition, the fabricated CD/CMCO heterojunctions also exhibited enhanced NIR-II photothermal properties, which can easily achieve mild NIR-II PTT at a safe power density of 1.0 W/cm2 after irradiating for 3 min. Mild hyperthermia produced by CD/CMCO can further improve the sonodynamic and catalytic performances of heterojunctions, realizing the second-level amplification of ROS production owing to the relieving hypoxia and enhanced catalytic activity. Notably, CD/CMCO heterojunctions exhibit the enhanced triple enzyme-like catalytic activities including Fenton-like reaction to produce more ROS, catalytic degradation of H2O2 to O2 for enhancing SDT, and the elimination of GSH to regulate TME, ultimately achieving the third-level amplification of ROS production. By leveraging these beneficial characteristics, tumor growth was successfully inhibited through the CD/CMCO-mediated triple amplification of ROS generation for heterojunction-amplified and mild NIR-II hyperthermia-enhanced SDT and NCT.

The detailed synthesis procedure of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction can be found in the support information. Initially, ZIF-67 nanoparticles were synthesized through the stirring of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and 2-methylimidazole in methanol. TEM image presented in Fig. S1a revealed a uniform regular dodecahedral structure of our prepared ZIF-67 nanoparticles. The absorption spectrum exhibited a distinct absorption peak at approximately 600 nm (Fig. S1c). Then, ZIF-67 was etched using manganese nitrate tetrahydrate to form a Mn/ZIF-67 yolk-shell structure (Fig. S1b), during which the protons generated by Mn2+ hydrolysis would gradually etch the ZIF-67 template, while the released Co2+ may be partially oxidized by dissolved oxygen and nitrate ion in the solution. Co2+/Co3+ would co-precipitate with Mn2+ to form a Mn–Co LDH shell layer. The absorption spectrum of Mn/ZIF-67 was like that of ZIF-67 (Fig. S1c). After being heated in an air atmosphere, the ZIF-67/Mn–Co LDH core-shell nanoparticles underwent a thermal decomposition process, leading to the conversion of the ZIF-67 core into Co3O4 and the Mn–Co LDH shell into MnCo2O4 nanocages.

By utilizing trinitropyrene and polyethylenimine as precursors, CDs were successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal method, showcasing remarkable photothermal properties. Fig. S2a showed a TEM image of uniformly distributed carbon dots with an average diameter of 4.6 ± 1.1 nm. High-resolution TEM of CDs exhibited a lattice structure with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. S2b). Next, XRD was employed to detect a diffraction peak around 25° of CDs (Fig. 1 j), which corresponds to the [002] crystal face of the graphite-like structure [65–67]. From the full XPS spectrum (Fig. S3a), it can be seen that CDs mainly consist of C, N, and O elements, with their respective contents of 70.3 at.%, 17.7 at.%, and 12.0 at.%. In addition, high-resolution N 1s spectra reveal that the nitrogen element in CDs primarily exists in the form of graphitic nitrogen and pyridinic nitrogen (Fig. S3c). High-resolution C 1s and O1s spectra disclosed the presence of chemical bonds such as C–C, CC, C–N, C–O, and O–H in CDs (Figs. S3b and d), which was consistent with the results obtained from FTIR (Fig. 1l). The enrichment of positive charges (31.7 ± 0.9 mV) on the surface is mainly attributed to the protonation of pyridinic nitrogen (Fig. S4b).

Finally, the CMCO heterojunctions were mixed with the prepared CDs at room temperature to form the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions through the coordination of pyridine N with Co and Mn elements. Furthermore, ZIF-67 was subjected to individual thermal decomposition processes to yield Co3O4 nanocages (Fig. S5a), while the ZIF-67 core within ZIF-67/Mn–Co LDH was selectively etched away and subsequently thermally decomposed to produce MnCo2O4 nanocages (Fig. S5b). TEM images depicted in Fig. 1a, c revealed that the average particle size of the prepared CMCO and CD/CMCO is about 300–400 nm, which was consistent with their hydrodynamic diameters of 357 nm and 382 nm (Fig. S4a). TEM images also exhibited that both CMCO and CD/CMCO have typical shell-core hollow structure, and the gap between the inner shell of Co3O4 and the outer shell of MnCo2O4 can be clearly distinguished. The elemental mapping indicated that Co and O are uniformly distributed in the shell and core (Fig. 1m and S6), with a marked enhancement in the central region, while Mn is only present in the shell. The higher N element distribution on the surface of CD/CMCO indicates the successful adsorption of CDs. The lattice spacing of 0.25 nm displayed in the HR-TEM image of CMCO and CD/CMCO can be assigned to the (311) plane of cobalt oxide spinel Co3O4 (JCPDS NO.74-2120), while that of 0.16 nm, 0.20 nm, and 0.29 nm lattice spacings can be assigned to the manganese cobalt oxide spinel MnCo2O4.5 (JCPDS NO.32-0097) (Fig. 1b–d). Furthermore, the 0.21 nm interplanar spacing measured from the edge of the CMCO shell belonged to the (100) plane of CDs. The above structural characterization results were all consistent with the XRD patterns of CMCO and CD/CMCO (Fig. 1j and S7), and a weak peak corresponding to CDs at approximately 25° was also observed in the XRD pattern of CD/CMCO. When positively charged CDs were coordinated, the zeta potential of CMCO increased from 1.8 ± 0.5 mV to 28.4 ± 0.3 mV (Fig. S4b). Moreover, the characteristic absorption peaks of CDs (about 420 nm) and CMCO (about 780 nm) were also observed in that of CD/CMCO, clearly demonstrating the successful preparation of ternary heterojunctions.

XPS measurements were also conducted to investigate the chemical properties and composition of the prepared CD/CMCO. As shown in Fig. 1e, the XPS full spectrum analysis of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions revealed the presence of Mn, Co, O, N, and C elements. The high-resolution spectrum of the Mn 2p contained two adjacent peaks (Fig. 1f), which correspond to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2. The fitted peaks at 641.9 eV and 653.4 eV were attributed to Mn2+, while the other two peaks at 643.9 eV and 654.5 eV can be attributed to Mn3+. Similarly, in the high-resolution Co 2p spectrum (Fig. 1g), two signal peaks were shown at 779.2 eV and 794.4 eV, respectively, representing Co 2p2/3 and Co 2p1/2. By fitting, it was found that they can be divided into satellite peaks of Co2+ and Co3+. The O 1s spectrum exhibited excellent agreement with three peaks at 529.9, 531.1, and 532.4 eV (Fig. 1h), corresponding to the crystalline and amorphous O2− species as well as adsorbed H2O in CD/CMCO. Moreover, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum demonstrated that the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction exhibited distinct peaks corresponding to pyridine N, metal N, and graphitic N (Fig. 1i). Similarly, the three peaks in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum fitting are attributed to C–C/CC, C–N and C–OH respectively (Fig. S9e). These findings provided further evidence of the successful synthesis of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions. For comparison, the corresponding XPS spectrum of the single-component Co3O4 and MnCo2O4 can be found in Fig. S8, S9(a-d). Afterwards, we proceeded to investigate the stability of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction. Both CMCO and CD/CMCO were immersed in physiological saline solutions for observation of their colloidal stability. It was observed that the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions demonstrated enhanced stability compared with CMCO (Fig. S10a). The excellent colloidal stability of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions was further confirmed through analysis of its hydrodynamic diameter after storing for different times (Fig. S10b), indicating that no obvious size change of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction can be detected after 7 days.

After achieving successful synthesis of the trinary CD/CMCO heterojunctions, we investigated the sonodynamic activity of CD/CMCO by utilizing DPBF as a probe for produced ROS. In contrast to CMCO without CD (Fig. 2 a), the CD/CMCO system exhibited enhanced production of ROS when subjected to ultrasound (Fig. 2c), as demonstrated by the more prominent optical absorption of DPBF at 418 nm. The degradation rate constant of DPBF by CD/CMCO was determined to be 0.139 min−1, which was 2.1 times greater than that of CMCO (0.066 min−1) (Fig. 2d). Furthermore, the sonodynamic performance of CDs was evaluated under the same conditions, revealing negligible change in its optical absorption at 418 nm when irradiated by US (Fig. 2b), indicating a lack of ROS production capability. Therefore, the augmented SDT activity of CD/CMCO could be ascribed to the synergistic effect of the trinary heterojunctions. Additionally, to verify the generation of ROS, ESR measurements were carried out to determine the ROS production capacity of CD/CMCO, CMCO, and CD with TEMP as a ROS scavenger. The ESR signal of CD/CMCO, as illustrated in Fig. 2e, demonstrated a significantly enhanced intensity when contrasted with CMCO. These findings unequivocally demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs has substantially augmented the sonodynamic performance of CMCO, potentially attributable to the enhancement of charge carrier transfer within the trinary heterojunctions. To understand why the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions exhibited enhanced sonodynamic performance, we analyzed the bandgap widths of Co3O4, MnCo2O4, and CDs using the Tauc plot method. The calculations revealed values of 1.88 eV, 2.10 eV, and 2.20 eV, respectively (as depicted in Fig. 2f). Moreover, the XPS-VB analysis indicated VB values of 0.20 eV, 0.52 eV, and 1.64 eV for Co3O4, MnCo2O4, and CDs, respectively (Fig. 2g–i). Based on these data points, a band diagram of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions was constructed (Fig. 2j). Under US irradiation, Co3O4, MnCo2O4, and CDs generate electrons and holes in the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. This tri-component system involves two electron-hole transfer processes: (1) the recombination of conduction band electrons in CDs with valence band holes in MnCo2O4; (2) the recombination of electrons in the conduction band of MnCo2O4 with vacant holes in the valence band of Co3O4. By facilitating two charge transfer processes at the non-homogeneous interface, the lifetime of charge carriers was significantly prolonged and electron-hole recombination was effectively inhibited. Due to the significantly lower conduction band potential (−1.68 eV) on the Co3O4 side compared to the reduction potential of O2/•O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE), this makes the formation of 1O2 easier from a thermodynamic perspective.

Due to the advantageous optical absorption properties of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions in the near-infrared region, we proceeded to explore its photothermal capabilities within this spectral range. Considering the maximum allowable irradiation powers of near-infrared regions I and II at 0.42 and 1.0 W/cm2, respectively, we exposed 1.0 mL of CD/CMCO water solution to 808 and 1064 nm lasers at power densities of 0.3 and 1.0 W/cm2, respectively. Fig. 3 k and S11 demonstrated that both laser-irradiated CD/CMCO solutions can readily attain temperatures exceeding 50 °C after 10 min of irradiation, indicating the commendable photothermal performance of CD/CMCO under permissible irradiation power. Furthermore, a comparative analysis of temperature variations among CD/CMCO, CMCO, and CDs under identical laser irradiation conditions revealed that the incorporation of CDs enhanced the photothermal performance of CMCO to a certain extent (Fig. 3g and h). We subsequently examined the photothermal performance of varying concentrations of CD/CMCO solution under laser irradiation, as illustrated in Fig. 3b–d. Our findings indicated a direct correlation between the concentration of CD/CMCO solution and the resulting temperature increase during laser irradiation, thus demonstrating the concentration-dependent nature of CD/CMCO's photothermal performance. Fig. 3a, c illustrated the temperature variations of a specific concentration of CD/CMCO solution under varying power densities of laser irradiation, demonstrating that the photothermal performance of CD/CMCO is also influenced by the laser power density. Furthermore, in addition to exhibiting excellent photothermal performance, CD/CMCO demonstrated remarkable photothermal stability (Fig. 3e and f). Subsequently, we conducted measurements of photothermal conversion efficiency to showcase the potential of CD/CMCO as an excellent NIR-II PTT agent. Based on the experiment data, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CD/CMCO was found to be 78.9 % and 50.1 % under 808 or 1064 nm laser irradiation. The outstanding photothermal performance of CD/CMCO was clearly demonstrated by its excellent photothermal conversion efficiency in both the NIR-I and NIR-II windows. Furthermore, at a radiation power of 1.0 W/cm2 and a radiation time of 3 min, mild NIR-II PTT can be obtained from CD/CMCO, raising the temperature of CD/CMCO to about 43 °C.

Since Co2+ and Mn2+ were presented in the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions, TMB was used as a probe to study the POD-like catalytic activity of CD/CMCO. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, an increase in H2O2 concentration at pH 6.0 resulted in a significant enhancement of absorbance at 652 nm for both CMCO and CD/CMCO. Additionally, the rate constants for •OH generation were determined to be 0.351 min−1 for CD/CMCO and 0.292 min−1 for CMCO (Fig. S12). In contrast, CD alone failed to catalyze H2O2 into •OH (Fig. S13). At pH 6.5, there was a reduction in •OH generation rates for both CD/CMCO and CMCO (Fig. 4e and f), suggesting that these samples possess pH-dependent capabilities for •OH production. Notably, no significant •OH generation was detected in either CD/CMCO or CMCO at pH 7.4 (Fig. S14), implying that neither is likely to exhibit toxicity towards normal tissues under physiological conditions. Furthermore, utilizing DMPO as a trapper for •OH allowed us to confirm the remarkable POD-like enzymatic catalytic activity of both CD/CMCO and CMCO (Fig. S15).

Given that temperature can influence enzyme-like catalytic activity, we further examined how mild photothermal effects impact the catalytic performance of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions. As depicted in Fig. 4c–g, TMB absorbance levels were higher in the CD/CMCO +1064 nm group compared with those in the CD/CMCO alone group across both pH values of 6.0 and 6.5. Furthermore, the catalytic performance of the samples was rigorously assessed through enzyme kinetic analysis. In a pH 6.0 environment, the K M value for the CD/CMCO +1064 nm group was determined to be lower (0.83 mM) while its V max value was higher (2.70 × 10−7 M s−1) in comparison to the CD/CMCO group (1.07 mM, 1.74 × 10−7 M s−1) and the CMCO group (1.13 mM, 1.38 × 10−7 M s−1) (Fig. 4d). These findings suggested that the POD-like catalytic activity of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction can be enhanced by mild NIR-II photothermal effects. Similarly, under a pH 6.5 condition, both materials exhibited increased K M values alongside decreased V max (Fig. 4h). Collectively, these results demonstrated that the engineered ternary heterojunctions significantly enhanced catalytic activity and that this enhancement can be further augmented through irradiation with mild 1064 nm laser.

Tumor cells with elevated levels of GSH can defend against oxidative stress by eliminating ROS, thus reducing the effectiveness of ROS-based sonodynamic therapy. The presence of Co3+ and Mn3+ in CD/CMCO raised the question of whether CD/CMCO exhibited GSH-px-like catalytic activity. Following incubation with GSH for varying durations, a marked reduction in the characteristic absorption peak of DTNB was observed in both CD/CMCO and CMCO solutions (Fig. 4i and S16a), indicating that GSH was consumed by both CD/CMCO and CMCO, while CD alone did not exhibit such consumption (Fig. S17). After an incubation period of 2.5 h, nearly all GSH was depleted by CD/CMCO and CMCO (Fig. 4j and S16b).

We then investigated the CAT-like catalytic activity of CD/CMCO, CMCO, and CD under conditions of pH 6.0 and H2O2 concentration at 0.4 mM. As illustrated in Fig. S18, CMCO exhibited a slightly lower oxygen generation capacity compared to CD/CMCO, while CD did not demonstrate significant CAT catalytic activity. To further assess the oxygen-generating capability of CD/CMCO and CMCO, we mixed them with varying concentrations of H2O2 and measured the resultant oxygen content produced by each sample over an 8-min period (Fig. 4k and S19). These findings indicated that CD/CMCO possesses superior CAT-like catalytic activity and exhibited a dependence on H2O2 concentration. Furthermore, the oxygen-generation proficiency of CD/CMCO was appraised under disparate environmental circumstances (pH 6.0, 6.5, and 7.4). As depicted in Fig. S20, at pH 6.0, CD/CMCO manifested an augmented capacity to interact with H2O2 to engender augmented O2 output. The higher CAT-like catalytic activity of CD/CMCO could be ascribed to the protonation of pyridine nitrogen groups, which made CDs possessed the positively charged features. This phenomenon could facilitate the adsorption of hydroxy group on the surface of nanozymes, thereby enhancing the CAT-like catalytic activity of CD/CMCO at pH 6.0. Through the computation of their catalytic efficacy (Fig. 4l), it becomes conspicuous that CD/CMCO exhibited a higher V max (2.7 × 10−6 M s−1) and a lower K M (0.7 mM). These results demonstrated that the acceleration of electron transfer processes contributes to the preeminent catalytic activity of the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions.

Leveraging the exceptional sonodynamic, photothermal properties, and nanocatalytic activity of CD/CMCO, we investigated the synergistic antitumor effects of sonodynamic-photothermal enhanced nanocatalytic treatment at the cellular level. LO2 and CT26 served as model systems for this study. Various concentrations of CD/CMCO, CMCO, and CDs were co-incubated with LO2 cells and no significant alterations in cell viability were observed, indicating that all three compounds exhibit favorable biocompatibility (Fig. 5 a and S21). Fig. 5b delineated the cell viability of CT26 cells after treatment with disparate concentrations of CD/CMCO for periods of 24 h and 48 h. The data evidenced a negative correlation between the concentration of CD/CMCO and the survival rate of CT26 cells; precisely, at a concentration of 200 μg/mL following an incubation period of 48 h, the cell survival rate approximates 70 %. In contrast, the survival rate for CT26 cells subjected to identical circumstances with CMCO is higher (75 %) (Fig. S22a), further substantiating that the CD/CMCO ternary heterojunction exhibited superior POD-like catalytic activity. Significantly, CD alone does not exert any toxic ramifications on CT26 cells (Fig. S22b). There is a pronounced decrement in cell viability post-ultrasound irradiation, particularly at a concentration of 200 μg/mL over an incubation time of 48 h, where cell survival plummets to merely 27 % (Fig. 5c). For the combined SDT and mild photothermal enhancement via CD/CMCO in vitro, each well was irradiated with a 1064 nm laser prior to ultrasound exposure (0.3 W/cm2 for 3 min). This approach engendered an even more considerable decline in cell viability, culminating in a final survival rate as low as 12 %. The viability outcomes for both groups treated with equivalent concentrations of CMCO and subsequently exposed to ultrasound and laser irradiation are presented in Fig. S23. These findings disclose that under comparable experimental conditions, the survival rate among those treated solely with CMCO surpasses that observed within the CD/CMCO group, indicating that the heterojunction and mild hyperthermia co-amplified NCT elicited optimal antitumor effectiveness.

To further expound upon the antitumor potency of the CD/CMCO-mediated synergistic tumor therapy, live/dead staining was executed on CT26 cells subjected to diverse treatments. As presented in Fig. 5e, an increased number of dead cells were detected in the CD/CMCO-treated CT26 cells, intimating that CD/CMCO may interact with intracellular excessive H2O2 to exert definite antitumor effects. The fluorescence images from the CMCO + US treatment group displayed an augmentation in red fluorescence representative of dead cells. A marginal fraction of CT26 cells remained viable after treatment with CD/CMCO + US, suggesting that this combination can effectively induce cytotoxicity via the generation of an elevated level of ROS under ultrasound stimulation. Remarkably, image of the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 nm group disclosed an insignificant amount of green fluorescence corresponding to live cells; this indicated that the majority of CT26 cells were eradicated under these conditions, underscoring the outstanding therapeutic efficacy of the CD/CMCO-mediated heterojunction and mild hyperthermia co-amplified NCT.

Subsequently, we utilized the DCFH-DA probe to perform staining on CT26 cells that underwent disparate treatments for the purpose of evaluating intracellular ROS levels. As depicted in Fig. 5f, no perceptible green fluorescence signal was discerned in CT26 cells treated with PBS or US alone, signifying that US treatment failed to generate ROS sufficient to induce cell death. In contrast, a mild green fluorescence signal representative of ROS generation was detected in the CD/CMCO group alone, which was ascribed to the POD-like catalytic reaction mediated by Co2+ and Mn2+. The ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction manifested a more intense green fluorescence signal compared to the dual-component CMCO under US irradiation. Of particular significance, the most robust green fluorescence signal was registered in the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 nm group, suggesting that the ROS produced by the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction in the presence of a mild 1064 nm laser and US irradiation exceeded that of other groups. In addition, we performed cell apoptosis assay using Annexin V-FITC and PI as the dyes. As depicted in Fig. S24, the most apoptotic cells can be observed in the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 group, demonstrating the ROS-induced cell apoptosis. Furthermore, we also conducted JC-1 staining to investigate mitochondrial damage. Fig. 5g exhibited that the most serious mitochondrial damage can be found in the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 group, indicating that CD/CMCO-mediated combination therapy could induce cell apoptosis and cause mitochondrial damage. These outcomes further validated that the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunction exhibited extraordinary therapeutic effects through heterojunction and mild hyperthermia co-amplified NCT.

Inspired by the outstanding results of the in vitro experiments, we then investigated the synergistic antitumor effect of CD/CMCO. We initially evaluated the passive targeting efficiency of CD/CMCO via NIR imaging. As depicted in Fig. 6 b–d, a gradual intensification of fluorescence was witnessed after intravenous injection of ICG-labeled CD/CMCO. The ex vivo NIR fluorescence imaging of major organs and tumors further substantiated the tumor accumulation potentiality of CD/CMCO, with the maximum accumulation being detectable 24 h post-injection (Fig. 6c–e). Based on these discoveries, the optimum time point for ultrasound irradiation was identified as 24 h after the injection of CD/CMCO (Fig. 6a). Subsequently, we probed into the anti-tumor potency of CD/CMCO-mediated synergistic tumor therapy. In contrast to the control group and the sole US group, the tumor growth was partially repressed in the CD/CMCO alone group (Fig. 6g and h), signifying that the nanocatalytic activity of CD/CMCO possessed a certain antitumor attribute. Furthermore, the CD/CMCO + US group manifested a pronounced inhibitory impact on tumor growth. The incorporation of mild 1064 nm laser irradiation further mitigated tumor growth, which can be ascribed to the enhanced SDT along with the mild photothermal-enhanced NCT. To further illuminate the exceptional synergistic sonodynamic efficacy conferred by photothermal enhancement through CD/CMCO, mice were sacrificed at 24 h post-treatment for the collection of tumors subjected to H&E and TUNEL staining assays. Fig. 6j, k demonstrated that the tumors in the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 nm group sustained the most severe damage. Additionally, ROS staining disclosed that this treatment cohort detected the highest ROS signal (Fig. S25). Collectively, these outcomes forcefully substantiated that the construction of ternary heterojunctions and the employment of mild hyperthermia conspicuously elevated both SDT and NCT efficiency. Ultimately, the in vivo biocompatibility of CD/CMCO-mediated synergistic tumor therapy was assessed through weight change measurements and histological analyses of the mice. Throughout the 14-day treatment duration, the body weight of mice across all treatment groups remained relatively stable (Fig. 6f). Additionally, Fig. S26 illustrated that no significant pathological alterations were detected in the major organs of mice from any group, thereby indicating that the trinary CD/CMCO heterojunction exhibited favorable biocompatibility in vivo. We also performed the blood routine and biochemical analysis of the mice that were sacrificed at 14 days after different treatments. As presented in Fig. S27, the markers of blood biochemistry and blood routine examination in the CD/CMCO + US + 1064 group were all within normal ranges, suggesting that CD/CMCO-mediated combination therapy possessed excellent biosafety.

In this study, we successfully developed a tri-component heterojunction nanozyme exhibiting exceptional photothermal properties, sonodynamic characteristics, and multi-enzyme mimetic activity. As an effective nanozyme, the ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions leveraged the mediating effects of Co2+ and Mn2+ to demonstrate catalytic efficacy akin to that of peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT), thereby generating •OH and O2. Additionally, CD/CMCO utilized Co3+ and Mn3+ to eliminate excess GSH while promoting the accumulation of ROS. Furthermore, owing to the enhanced electronic properties conferred by the heterojunction structure, this tri-component CD/CMCO acted as a more potent ROS generator. More interestingly, the mild photothermal effect further augmented the catalytic efficiency of the nanozymes, significantly enhancing tumor treatment efficacy mediated by ROS. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments corroborated the biocompatibility as well as substantial antitumor effects of CD/CMCO, underscoring its considerable potential as an innovative sonodynamic-photothermal enhanced nanocatalytic synergistic therapeutic agent for tumor treatment.

The precursor 1,3,6-trinitropyrene was synthesized via a nitration reaction. Specifically, 4 g of pyrene was dissolved in 320 mL of concentrated nitric acid and maintained at 80 °C for a duration of two days. Subsequently, the unreacted pyrene and excess nitric acid were removed through repeated filtration until the filtration reaches neutrality.

The preparation of CDs involved the following steps: Initially, 0.8 g of polyethylenimine (Mw = 1800) was accurately weighed and sonicated in 40 mL of deionized water until fully dissolved. Subsequently, 0.1 g of TNP was introduced and subjected to sonication until complete dissolution occurs. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a 100 mL PTFE reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for a duration of 10 h under autoclave conditions. Finally, CDs were purified via dialysis using a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 Da before being utilized in subsequent experiments.

160 mg of tetrahydrate manganese nitrate was dissolved in 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol under stirring until complete dissolution occurred. Following this, 80 mg of ZIF-67 was introduced into the solution, and sonication was performed for 10 min to ensure thorough dispersion of ZIF-67. The resulting mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 30 min to facilitate the formation of ZIF-67/Mn–Co LDH. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture underwent centrifugation and was washed three times each with water and ethanol before being dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven for subsequent applications. The dried ZIF-67/Mn–Co LDH was subsequently placed in a tube furnace and subjected to heating in an air atmosphere (350 °C, 2 h) to yield CMCO.

The ternary CD/CMCO heterojunctions were prepared by stirring a mass ratio of mCD:mCMCO = 2:1 in aqueous phase overnight. After completion of this reaction, the resultant mixture underwent centrifugation and multiple washes with water and ethanol before being stored in aqueous phase for future use.

TEM images of CD/CMCO, CMCO and CDs were acquired by a JEOL-JEM-F200 microscope. The valence of elements in CDs, CMCO, and CD/CMCO were measured by XPS. 1O2 of CDs, CMCO and CD/CMCO were detected by Bruker EMX plus ESR spectrometer. Absorbance spectrum was recorded by Hitachi 3100. The Malvern Nano ZS90 analyzer was utilized to acquire the hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential of CDs, CMCO, and CD/CMCO. The Leica TCS SP5 type laser confocal microscope was employed for the experiments of live/dead cell staining and ROS staining.

For photothermal property measurements, the 1 mL solutions of CD, CMCO, and CD/CMCO were irradiated with 808 nm and 1064 nm lasers for 10 min. Regarding the concentration-dependent photothermal behavior, the concentration of CD/CMCO ranged from 0 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL. For sonodynamic property measurements, 60 μL of DPBF was introduced into 3 mL of a CD/CMCO aqueous solution in darkness, followed by ultrasound irradiation at a frequency of 50 kHz with an intensity of 3.0 W/cm2. The absorbance of DPBF at the characteristic peak wavelength of 418 nm was recorded every minute over a duration of 5 min to assess the ROS generation from CD/CMCO. For the measurements of photothermal conversion efficiency, the CD/CMCO aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) was irradiated by 808 nm and 1064 nm lasers. The temperature changes of the solution within 10 min were recorded by Fluke infrared thermal imager (recorded once every 60 s). The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of CD/CMCO was calculated by Equation (1).

According to the calculation, the photothermal conversion efficiencies of CD/CMCO at 808 nm and 1064 nm are 78.9 % and 50.1 % respectively.

For POD-like catalytic activity, adding 10 μL of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to 100 μL of CD, CMCO, or CD/CMCO solution. Subsequently, introducing the resulting mixture into 3 mL of varying concentrations of H2O2 (0.00, 0.25, 0.50, 1.00, and 2.00 mM) within an acetic acid-sodium acetate (NaAc-HAc) buffer solution at pH values of 6.0, 6.5, or 7.4. The absorbance at a wavelength of 652 nm was measured by absorption spectrophotometer. For GSH-px-like catalytic activity, utilizing DTNB as an indicator and combining 1 mM GSH with CD/CMCO (500 μg/mL) in a volume ratio of 1:1. Subsequently, at various time intervals, withdrawing 100 μL of the resulting mixture and adding it to 10 μL of DTNB (0.2 mM) in a total volume of 900 μL PBS. The consumption of GSH by CD/CMCO can be assessed by measuring the absorbance of the resultant solution at 412 nm. For CAT-like catalytic activity, varying concentrations of H2O2 solution were combined with a CD/CMCO solution. The oxygen production in the mixture was measured using the dissolved oxygen meter.

LO2 and CT26 were procured from the Institute of Cell Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences. For the MTT assay, LO2 or CT26 cells were plated in 96-well plates. Following cell adhesion to the plate, varying concentrations of CD, CMCO, and CD/CMCO samples were introduced into the wells and incubated for either 24 or 48 h. To evaluate in vitro SDT and mild photothermal-enhanced NCT, each well was subjected to ultrasound irradiation at a frequency of 50 kHz with an intensity of 3.0 W/cm2 or exposed to a laser at a wavelength of 1064 nm with an intensity of 1.0 W/cm2 for a duration of 3 min, followed by subsequent MTT analysis.

Following the seeding of CT26 cells in 6-well plates and a 24-h incubation period, the cells in each well were treated with specific materials as per the experimental protocol (control, US, CD/CMCO, CMCO + US, CD/CMCO + US, CD/CMCO + US+1064 nm) for an additional 24 h. Notably, treatments were administered to the cells in different wells at intervals of 4 h according to the established protocol. Subsequently, all wells underwent dual staining using Calcein-AM/PI to differentiate between live and dead cells, which were then observed and photographed under a fluorescence microscope. The ROS levels in CT26 cells were assessed using an ROS detection kit (Biyuntian, China) following various treatments. Briefly, CT26 cells were plated in confocal dishes and incubated overnight. Subsequently, they underwent treatment according to the experimental protocol (Control, US, CD/CMCO, CMCO + US, CD/CMCO + US, CD/CMCO + US+1064 nm). Finally, the cells were stained with DCFH-DA probe and analyzed using a confocal microscope to capture images.

CT-26 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well in a confocal dish and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, six different treatment methods (Group 1: control, Group 2: US, Group 3: CD/CMCO, Group 4: CMCO + US, Group 5: CD/CMCO + US, Group 6: CD/CMCO + US+1064 nm) were applied. After adding the materials for 4 h, US treatment (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm−2, 50 % duty cycle) was applied to the corresponding groups, and then incubation was continued for 12 h. Finally, the cells were stained with the JC-1 mitochondrial membrane potential detection kit (Biyun Tian) and imaged by CLSM.

Apoptosis was evaluated in vitro by Annexin V-FITC/PI staining method. 1 × 105 CT-26 cells were seeded in 6-well plates and cultured at 37 °C for 24 h. After different treatments, the cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC apoptosis detection kit (Biyun Tian) and analyzed by flow cytometry.

In vivo NIR imaging experiments were conducted to evaluate the passive targeting capability of CD/CMCO. ICG was incubated with CD/CMCO for 24 h to produce ICG-CD/CMCO for in vivo imaging applications. Following this, the formulation was administered via tail vein injection into tumor-bearing mice. Subsequently, in vivo NIR fluorescence imaging was performed using the IVIS Lumina III in vivo imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA) at various time points (0, 4, 8, 12, 24, and 48 h). Furthermore, at designated time points, one mouse from each group was euthanized to collect major organs and tumors for ex vivo NIR imaging.

Five-week-old female Balb/c mice were obtained from Shanghai Schleicher Experimental Animal Co., Ltd. All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the guidelines approved by the Shanghai University Animal Care and Use Institutional Committee (SYXK2019-0020). A tumor model was established through subcutaneous injection of 1 × 106 CT26 cells into the axillary region of the mice. The mice were then randomly assigned to six groups. Each group received distinct treatments: Group 1: intravenous saline injection as a control; Group 2: intravenous saline injection followed by 10 min of ultrasound irradiation; Group 3: intravenous administration of CD/CMCO aqueous solution; Group 4: intravenous CMCO injection accompanied by 10 min of ultrasound irradiation; Group 5: intravenous CD/CMCO administration combined with 10 min of ultrasound irradiation; and Group 6: intravenous CD/CMCO treatment along with both 10 min of ultrasound irradiation and 3 min of laser irradiation at a wavelength of 1064 nm. For histological analysis, tumors from each group were harvested and sectioned for ROS staining, H&E staining, and TUNEL staining.

All experiments are performed with at least three independent replicates. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). All statistical analysis is performed by Origin 2018, Excel, and Graphpad Prism 8.0. Statistical significance between the experimental group and the control group is calculated with a two-tailed Student's t-test. ∗ denotes a statistical significance (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, and ∗∗∗p < 0.001) between the data of the experimental group and the control group.
